title: SEGMENT 3 — The four mechanisms of resistance, recap and deepening

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The four mechanisms of resistance, recap and deepening
If you sat through the antibiotics lecture, you saw a quick survey of resistance mechanisms at the end.
Today we are going to go through them again, more slowly, and we are going to anchor each one to specific named examples that you will encounter in the literature and in the clinic.
There are, broadly, four mechanisms by which bacteria become resistant to antibiotics, and essentially every resistance phenotype in nature is some version of one of these, or a combination.
Mechanism one: pump the drug back out.
This is the efflux pump strategy.

A bacterium can express a membrane transporter that grabs the antibiotic — sometimes any of a wide range of toxic molecules, sometimes a specific class — and physically expels it from the cytoplasm back into the surrounding medium, against a concentration gradient, usually powered by ATP hydrolysis or by the proton motive force across the membrane.
The drug is in the cell briefly, the pump shoves it out, the steady-state intracellular concentration stays below the level needed to bind the target.
The drug literally cannot accumulate.
The target is undefended only because the bullet keeps getting bounced back outside the building.
Specific named pumps to know.

TetA, the tetracycline efflux pump, is the classic narrow-spectrum example — it's quite specific for tetracyclines, it sits in the inner membrane of gram-negative bacteria, it uses proton symport to extrude the drug.

AcrAB-TolC in E. coli and other gram-negatives is the canonical multi-drug efflux pump, and it is genuinely beautiful structural biology — three proteins forming a tunnel that spans both the inner and outer membranes, with AcrB in the inner membrane recognizing a startlingly broad range of substrates (beta-lactams, fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines, chloramphenicol, even bile salts and detergents) and TolC providing the outer-membrane channel.
It is a tripartite molecular vacuum cleaner.
NorA in Staph aureus pumps out fluoroquinolones.
MexAB-OprM in Pseudomonas is the AcrAB-TolC analog and is one of the major reasons Pseudomonas aeruginosa is hard to treat.
The efflux mechanism is everywhere, and what makes it particularly nasty is that a single multi-drug pump like AcrAB-TolC can confer modest resistance to many drug classes at once.
Upregulate AcrAB-TolC by a few-fold, and your MICs for half a dozen drugs all creep up.
The bug doesn't have to acquire a specific defense; it just turns up the general-purpose detoxifier.
Mechanism two: change the target.
The antibiotic works by binding some specific bacterial molecule — a transpeptidase, a ribosomal RNA position, a topoisomerase, an RNA polymerase subunit.

If the bacterium changes that molecule's shape just enough that the drug no longer binds tightly, but the molecule still does its physiological job, the drug becomes ineffective and the bacterium is fine.
This is the lock-changing strategy.
The drug arrives intact.
The target has been modified.
Affinity drops.
Sometimes ten-fold, sometimes ten-thousand-fold.
The bacterium continues to make peptidoglycan or read mRNA or supercoil DNA as if the drug weren't there.
Specific named examples.
The canonical one — and probably the single most clinically important target-modification resistance gene in the world — is mecA, in Staphylococcus aureus.
The mecA gene encodes a modified transpeptidase called PBP2a, an alternative penicillin-binding protein with a low affinity for essentially all beta-lactams.
A normal Staph aureus has four PBPs, all of which beta-lactams bind well.
Acquire mecA, and the cell now expresses a fifth one, PBP2a, that is functional — it can build cell wall — but is essentially invisible to methicillin, oxacillin, and most other beta-lactams.
The cell wall continues to be built even in the presence of high drug concentrations.
That single gene, on a mobile genetic element called SCCmec — staphylococcal cassette chromosome mec — is what defines MRSA, methicillin-resistant Staph aureus.
We'll come back to MRSA in detail in Segment 5.
Other target-modification examples.

Ribosomal mutations confer resistance to drugs that bind ribosomal RNA — a single point mutation in 23S rRNA can give you macrolide or linezolid resistance, because the drug-binding pocket is suddenly the wrong shape.

DNA gyrase mutations, specifically at certain key residues in the GyrA subunit (Ser83, Asp87 in E. coli), confer fluoroquinolone resistance — the drug used to wedge into the enzyme-DNA complex, and now the enzyme is shaped so the drug can't wedge.
RpoB mutations in Mycobacterium tuberculosis confer rifampin resistance — rifampin used to block bacterial RNA polymerase, and an altered RpoB no longer offers the binding pocket.
And in vancomycin resistance, which we'll cover in detail in Segment 6, the target itself — the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of the peptidoglycan precursor — is enzymatically rebuilt as D-Ala-D-Lac.
The drug used to bind the D-Ala-D-Ala.
The new D-Ala-D-Lac it cannot bind.
The lock has been changed at the molecular level.
Mechanism three: destroy the drug.
This is the enzymatic-degradation strategy, and it is the single most clinically prevalent resistance mechanism in the world by sheer headcount.
The bacterium expresses an enzyme that chemically modifies the antibiotic, rendering it inactive, before the drug can engage its target.
The drug is destroyed, often catalytically — a single enzyme molecule can chew up many drug molecules.
The cell is, effectively, secreting a counter-weapon.
The headline example, by far, is the beta-lactamase family.
These are enzymes that hydrolyze the beta-lactam ring — the four-membered ring at the heart of every penicillin, cephalosporin, and carbapenem.
Crack that ring open and the drug becomes a chemically inert decomposition product that the transpeptidase doesn't even recognize.
There are thousands of beta-lactamase variants in the wild, and they are evolving as we speak.
We're going to do a deep dive on beta-lactamases in the next segment because they deserve it.
But you should also know about aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes — AMEs — a family of bacterial enzymes that attach chemical groups to aminoglycoside antibiotics.
There are three subtypes: acetyltransferases (which add an acetyl group, AAC), phosphotransferases (which add a phosphate, APH), and nucleotidyltransferases (which add an AMP-like group, ANT).
Different enzymes hit different positions on different aminoglycosides, but the strategy is identical: chemically tag the drug, the modified drug no longer binds the ribosome, the bacterium survives.
Chloramphenicol acetyltransferase, CAT, acetylates chloramphenicol.
And — the modern terror — macrolide esterases, tetracycline destructases, and others, each evolved to inactivate its specific class.
Wherever there is an antibiotic, evolution will find a way to chemically destroy it.
Mechanism four: keep the drug out.

This is reduced permeability — the bacterium reduces the rate at which the antibiotic enters the cell, so even if there's drug in the surrounding medium, the intracellular concentration stays below MIC.
The standard version, in gram-negative bacteria, is porin loss.

Recall from the antibiotics lecture: gram-negative bacteria have an outer membrane that is mostly impermeable, and small hydrophilic antibiotics (most beta-lactams, many other drugs) get into the cell through specific channels called porins.

If the bacterium reduces the expression of a porin — say, OmpF or OmpC in E. coli, or OprD in Pseudomonas — the doorways narrow, fewer drug molecules get in per unit time, and the intracellular concentration drops.
Combined with even modest beta-lactamase activity, porin loss can push MICs from susceptible to resistant.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa becoming carbapenem-resistant frequently does so by losing OprD, the porin through which carbapenems normally enter.
The drug is in the medium, the cell is in the medium, but the cell wall is essentially locked.
There's a subtler version, which is target overproduction — the bacterium produces so much of the target that the drug cannot saturate all of it, and enough unbound target is left to do the job.
Sulfonamide resistance can work this way: produce so much dihydropteroate synthase that even saturating drug leaves some functional enzyme.

And there's biofilm formation, which is sort of a fifth quasi-mechanism — bacteria living in a biofilm matrix are physiologically slow-growing and partly drug-impermeable, and their effective MICs in vivo are dramatically higher than what you measure on planktonic cells in broth.
I'll mention biofilms again later when we talk about hospital infections.
So — four mechanisms.
Pump it out.
Change the lock.
Destroy the drug.
Block the door.

Write those down the side of your page, because for the rest of this lecture, every resistance story I tell you is going to slot into one of those four categories, and often, the most worrying resistant organisms combine more than one.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa at its worst has multidrug efflux pumps, a chromosomal beta-lactamase, porin loss, and modified targets, all at once.
That is why pan-resistant Pseudomonas infections are so unbelievably hard to treat: the bug has every defense the bacterial world has ever evolved, layered on top of each other.
Now let's go deep on the single most important resistance enzyme family in the world.
